summaryTemplate: {{className}} is the {{patternRole}} in this Builder implementation. The fluent step methods accumulate state across calls and the terminator emits the finished product. Pattern body lives at {{file}}:{{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}.
chosenRationaleTemplate: Tagged as Builder because {{className}} carries the three structural fingerprints together: a class scope (lines {{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}), at least one setter that returns *this by reference (chainable step), and a non-setter terminator method that returns the assembled product. A pure Factory Method or Method Chaining shape would be missing one of the three.

builder_class.note: Builder class boundary — owns the partial product state that survives across step calls. The pattern is built around state accumulation here, not single-call construction.

fluent_setter_return.note: Step return signature — returning a reference (not a copy) is what makes step chaining safe; each step mutates the same builder instance instead of forking a new one per call.

fluent_self_return.note: `return *this` — this is the structural commitment to chaining. Without it, each step call would dead-end and the user would have to assign the builder to a fresh variable between steps.

builder_terminator_method.note: Terminator — separates the *accumulating* phase (setters) from the *emitting* phase (this method). Calling it before all required state is set is the typical Builder misuse the pattern is designed to flag.

runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Factory Method: Factory Method also returns a constructed product, but it does so in a single call without step accumulation. {{className}} carries setter-then-terminator structure (lines {{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}); collapsing it to a Factory would lose the per-step configurability.
runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Method Chaining: Method Chaining shares the `return *this` signature but does not require a distinct terminator that emits a product. {{className}} has the terminator, so the chain is purposeful rather than purely fluent.
runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Prototype: Prototype clones an existing instance; Builder assembles a new one step by step. {{className}} accumulates state into a fresh product, which is the Builder distinction.
